notes: Keyword/regex rubric for programmatically classifying ~56K real ATS job posting titles into the 18 SWE Genie archetypes (see taxonomy/archetypes.json for canonical ids/names, docs/research/roles-summary.md and docs/research/roles/*.md for scope). All patterns are Python `re` syntax, intended to be compiled with re.IGNORECASE. Application order: for each title, iterate archetypes in ascending `priority` order; for the first archetype whose title matches ANY of its positive_patterns AND matches NONE of its negative_patterns, assign that archetype. If a title matches an entry in always_exclude_patterns, do not classify it into any archetype regardless of priority order (check this first, before the priority loop). If a title exactly (or near-exactly, ignoring level suffixes like II/III/Senior) matches an entry in known_ambiguous_titles, route it to manual/sample review instead of auto-classifying, even if a positive_patterns regex would otherwise match. This is a precision-over-recall rubric: tighter patterns and more manual-review flags are preferred over broad recall, per product requirement (a wrong classification pollutes assessment credibility).

archetypes.disambiguation_note: Distinct from Consulting Engineer/Professional Services (FDE writes and owns production code shipped into the customer's environment, is not billable-utilization-comped, and doesn't carry a SOW end date) and from Solutions Architect (FDE codes 70-90% of the week vs. an SA's design/advisory focus). The bare acronym 'FDE' has no other common meaning in software job titles so is safe to match directly, unlike most 3-4 letter acronyms in this rubric. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found 'Forward Deployment Engineer' (Databricks) and 'AI Deployment Engineer, Enterprise' (OpenAI -- OpenAI's own name for its FDE-equivalent role, modeled on Palantir's) falling through to consulting-engineer-professional-services because the rubric only matched 'deployed', not the 'deployment' word-order variant; added both.

'Embedded Software Engineer' should match here, not the generic product-full-stack-software-engineer catch-all, hence its low (early-checked) priority number. Watch for 'Embedded' used as a business-context adjective unrelated to firmware (e.g. 'Embedded Finance Engineer', 'Embedded Analyst' at a client site) -- these are rare in practice but worth a spot-check during rollout since 'embedded' + a non-hardware noun can slip past the positive pattern's \b(engineer|developer)\b requirement only if the intervening word count is small; monitor false-positive rate. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found the reversed word order 'Software Engineer, Embedded Systems' / 'Software Engineer - Embedded Systems' (Roku, Applied Intuition) falling through to UNCLASSIFIED -- added a pattern for engineer/developer followed by 'embedded systems'.

Bare '\bmobile\b' is deliberately excluded from positive_patterns per the task's own example ('Mobile Sales Rep') -- it must co-occur with 'engineer'/'developer'/'app' or a mobile-specific platform token (ios/android/react native/flutter/swiftui). 'Mobile' alone is one of the highest false-positive-risk tokens in a mixed ATS title corpus (mobile crisis counselor, mobile notary, mobile home sales, mobile phlebotomist, mobile veterinary tech all appear in real ATS data).

'Production Engineer' and bare 'Reliability Engineer' are both genuinely overloaded with non-software usage (oil & gas, manufacturing, plant/process reliability engineering) -- see known_ambiguous_titles. Only 'Site Reliability Engineer' and the 'SRE' acronym are treated as unambiguous positives here; bare 'Production Engineer' is included as positive but guarded by the manufacturing/industrial negative-pattern list, and should still be spot-checked post-classification given the scale of industrial-sector postings in a generic ATS harvest. 'Customer Reliability Engineer' (Cloudflare-style) is a distinct boundary case -- see known_ambiguous_titles, not auto-routed here despite containing 'Reliability Engineer'.

Explicitly excludes Research/Applied Scientist (PhD-gated, publication-oriented, out of v1 scope per roles-summary.md) even though these titles sometimes appear adjacent to 'ML Engineer' postings at the same company -- see always_exclude_patterns. 'AI Engineer' is a fast-growing 2025-2026 title that's mostly a synonym for ML Engineer/LLM application engineer in practice, but watch for drift if 'AI Engineer' starts being used for non-technical AI-adjacent roles (AI product/policy) in the dataset.

'Analytics Engineer' is folded in here per data-engineer.md's own scope note (treated as a sub-specialty of data engineering, not a separate archetype). Distinguished from ML Engineer at the feature-pipeline boundary -- titles with both 'data' and 'ML/machine learning' should route to ml-engineer (checked at lower priority number / earlier), enforced here via the negative pattern.

Requires 'engineer' or 'architect' in the title, which naturally excludes 'Security Analyst'/'Security Specialist' titles (a real, more junior/adjacent title family this rubric deliberately does not claim). Offensive-security titles ('Penetration Tester', 'Red Team Engineer') are NOT included as positive matches here even though they're software-security-adjacent, because the source brief explicitly scopes this archetype as defensive/proactive-posture work 'not primarily offensive/hacking' -- see known_ambiguous_titles. Non-software 'Security Guard/Officer' titles are excluded via negative_patterns and also appear in always_exclude_patterns as a backstop.

Genuinely blurs with sre-production-engineer at smaller companies per platform-infrastructure-engineer.md's own scope note ('Platform Engineers do both DevOps and SRE work' at many shops) -- the negative pattern pushes titles with explicit SRE/reliability language to the SRE archetype (checked at an earlier priority number), which is the intended behavior, not a bug. 'Developer Experience Engineer' / 'DX Engineer' also collides with developer-relations-advocacy's DX sub-variant -- see known_ambiguous_titles. Bare 'Cloud Engineer' and bare 'Systems Engineer' are deliberately NOT included as positive patterns -- both are too overloaded across IT/ops/embedded/defense contexts to auto-classify; see known_ambiguous_titles. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found bare 'Infrastructure Engineer' matching semiconductor/chip-design-manufacturing titles at Intel/Micron ('DFT Verification Infrastructure Engineer', 'Design Infrastructure Engineer... Cell Characterization') -- these are hardware fab/silicon-process engineering roles, not software platform engineering; added semiconductor-manufacturing negative patterns.

Deliberately does NOT include bare 'Solutions Engineer' as a positive pattern, even though sales-engineer-pre-sales.md documents it as a common synonym at many B2B SaaS companies -- 'Solutions Engineer' alone is a genuine 3-way collision with solutions-architect-vendor-side and customer-support-solutions-engineer that title text cannot resolve without company/seniority/comp context. Bare 'Solutions Engineer' is routed to known_ambiguous_titles for manual review instead. 'Sales Engineer' and 'Presales Engineer' are unambiguous and safe to auto-classify directly. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found two real recall gaps: (1) 'Customer Engineer' (Google/Cloudflare's actual title for this exact role, e.g. 'Customer Engineer, India', 'Pre-Sales Customer Engineer') was entirely unmatched -- added, with an explicit negative-lookahead excluding any 'Post-Sales' qualified variant (which belongs to customer-support-solutions-engineer instead); (2) 'Solutions Consultant' (Ramp, Plaid, Oyster) is a very common real-world synonym for this role when NOT explicitly qualified 'Post Sales' -- added the same way, defaulting to pre-sales since that's the more common unqualified usage, while the explicitly post-sales-qualified variant routes to customer-support-solutions-engineer.

Vendor-side vs. consulting-side Solutions Architect cannot be reliably distinguished from title text alone -- both use the identical base title 'Solutions Architect' / 'Solution Architect'. This rubric resolves it only when the title carries an explicit vendor-product token (AWS/Azure/GCP/Salesforce/etc.) or an explicit consulting/SI qualifier (see solutions-architect-consulting); bare 'Solutions Architect' with neither signal is NOT auto-classified here -- see known_ambiguous_titles. Real-world ATS data will under-trigger this archetype relative to its true prevalence because company name (the strongest real-world signal) is not part of the title string in most postings.

CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found bare 'Enterprise Architect'/'Technology Architect' matching internal-corporate-IT roles at non-consultancy companies (GM, Micron, Disney, Humana, HPE) rather than actual consultancy-employed architects -- removed as unqualified positive signals, now require an explicit consulting/SI/client/delivery qualifier in the title itself, same as the other patterns. This sacrifices recall (won't catch a bare 'Enterprise Architect' title truly meant as consulting-side) in favor of precision, consistent with the rubric's stated philosophy. Bare 'Solutions Architect' remains intentionally unmatched by both SA archetypes -- see known_ambiguous_titles.

Distinguished from customer-support-solutions-engineer purely on the reactive/ticket-queue ('Support') vs. proactive/named-account ('Success'/'Solutions') keyword split documented in customer-support-solutions-engineer.md's own scope-decision section. 'Customer Reliability Engineer' (Cloudflare-style, SRE-staffed support role) is intentionally NOT matched by the plain 'support engineer' pattern and is not auto-classified here -- see known_ambiguous_titles.

'Technical Account Manager' (TAM) is folded in here as a synonym per customer-support-solutions-engineer.md, which found no evidence of a consistently distinct TAM archetype. Note the filename/in-document-title mismatch flagged in roles-summary.md ('customer-support-solutions-engineer.md' vs. in-document title 'Customer Success Engineer / Customer Solutions Engineer') is cosmetic and doesn't affect this rubric's id usage (uses the archetypes.json id, customer-support-solutions-engineer). CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found 'Solutions Consultant, Post Sales' (Ramp) -- a real, common post-sales-qualified variant -- was unmatched anywhere; added, paired with sales-engineer-pre-sales' complementary un-qualified/pre-sales version of the same pattern so the two archetypes split this title family consistently rather than either double-counting or dropping it. NOTE: the generic '\bsales\b' negative pattern would incorrectly exclude 'Post Sales' matches too since 'sales' is a substring -- added a fixed-width negative lookbehind so 'post-sales'/'post sales' doesn't trigger the exclusion; tested directly against both hyphen and space variants, confirmed correct.

Distinguished from Forward-Deployed Engineer (billable/SOW-scoped with a defined end date vs. FDE's open-ended embedded product-engineering ownership) and from Solutions Architect (implementation-led/hands-on vs. design-led/advisory) per consulting-engineer-professional-services.md's own scoping section. 'Technical Consultant' alone is a genuinely softer signal (could also describe a consulting-side Solutions Architect at more senior levels) -- flagged in known_ambiguous_titles for spot-review of the auto-classified population. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found (1) 'ASIC Implementation Engineer' (Broadcom, semiconductor chip design) false-positiving on the bare 'implementation engineer' pattern -- added semiconductor-context negative patterns; (2) bare 'deployment engineer' REMOVED as a positive pattern entirely -- a cross-company sample showed it means wildly different things depending on employer (OpenAI/Writer: FDE-equivalent AI deployment; Supabase/Crusoe/Anduril: infrastructure/network deployment tooling, closer to platform-infrastructure-engineer; Figure AI/Skydio/Path Robotics/Locus Robotics/Applied Intuition: physical robot/hardware site installation, arguably not a software archetype at all) -- no single archetype fits all of these, so it's now handled via company-context override (OpenAI specifically, whose 'AI Deployment Engineer' is publicly known to be modeled on Palantir's FDE) plus known_ambiguous_titles for the rest rather than a blanket regex guess.

'Developer Experience Engineer' / 'DX Engineer' is deliberately NOT included as a positive pattern here despite developer-relations-advocacy.md noting DX-titled roles sit 'under the broader DevRel umbrella' -- in practice this title is used as often for internal-platform-tooling work (which belongs to platform-infrastructure-engineer) as for external SDK/docs DevRel work. Left for known_ambiguous_titles rather than guessed either direction. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found bare 'Developer Relations' (Linear, WorkOS) and 'Director/Head, Developer Relations' (CoreWeave, Chainguard) falling through to UNCLASSIFIED because the pattern required an 'Engineer' suffix -- broadened to match 'Developer Relations' on its own.

Checked before the generalist product-full-stack-software-engineer archetype specifically because 'Engineering Manager' and 'Director of Engineering' both contain 'engineering' and would otherwise risk a loose generic-SWE pattern matching them. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found bare '\bcto\b' matching 'Executive Assistant to CTO' -- anchored the CTO pattern to only match when it's the leading token of the title (the actual CTO role, not a title that merely mentions a CTO), and added negative patterns for 'assistant/chief of staff/coordinator to' and 'to the CTO'. 'Tech Lead' / 'Team Lead' are deliberately NOT included as positive patterns -- these titles are genuinely split in real usage between IC-with-informal-leadership-duties (belongs in product-full-stack-software-engineer) and first-line people managers (belongs here), and the taxonomy's own source brief is explicit that EM is defined by evaluator-not-implementer status, which a bare 'Tech Lead' title doesn't reliably signal. See known_ambiguous_titles.

CRITICAL: 'Technical Product Manager' (TPM) and 'Technical Program Manager' (also commonly abbreviated TPM) are genuinely different roles that must NOT be merged -- Technical Product Manager owns a technical product's roadmap and makes architecture trade-off calls (per technical-product-manager.md); Technical Program Manager is a cross-team delivery/execution-coordination role closer to project management, not one of the 18 archetypes. The negative_pattern explicitly excludes 'Technical Program Manager'. The bare 'TPM' acronym is NOT used as a positive pattern anywhere in this rubric because it is genuinely ambiguous between the two meanings in real ATS data -- see known_ambiguous_titles. 'Product Owner' is also deliberately excluded from positive_patterns (often a Scrum-process role without the technical-architecture-tradeoff scope this archetype requires) -- see known_ambiguous_titles. CORRECTED after a precision spot-check found the original rubric's '\bproduct\s+manager,?\s*(api|platform|infrastructure|developer|technical)\b' pattern was matching generic 'Product Manager, Platform' / 'Product Manager, Infrastructure' / 'Product Manager, API' titles (Roku, Ripple, Grafana Labs, Coinbase, LayerZero -- 8 of 12 sampled matches) that don't say 'technical' anywhere -- these PMs own a technical product AREA but that's a topical/domain distinction, not evidence the person personally makes architecture trade-off calls, which is this archetype's actual defining trait per its own brief. Owning the Platform or API product area doesn't imply the 'technical' qualifier this archetype requires; removed the domain-keyword alternation and the bare 'API/Platform Product Manager' patterns entirely, now requires the literal word 'technical' adjacent to 'product manager'.

This is the generalist catch-all and MUST be checked last (highest priority number of all 18) -- the substring 'software engineer' or 'developer' appears inside the vast majority of the other 17 archetypes' real-world titles (Embedded Software Engineer, Security Software Engineer, Data Engineer -- wait, not that one, but Site Reliability Software Engineer, ML Software Engineer, etc.), so this archetype's negative_patterns list is intentionally the longest in the rubric, mirroring every other archetype's positive signal as a defensive exclusion. 'Software Engineer in Test' / 'SDET' / 'QA Engineer' are excluded here (and appear in always_exclude_patterns too) because no QA/test archetype exists among the 18 -- these titles should NOT be force-fit into the generalist bucket just because they contain 'software engineer'.

known_ambiguous_titles.reason: Explicitly documented in roles-summary.md's 'Naming ambiguity' section as a 3-way title collision used interchangeably across all three archetypes depending on company culture. Title text alone cannot resolve it; recommend routing to manual/sample review or using company-name/seniority/comp-band signals if available downstream.

known_ambiguous_titles.pattern: Solutions Architect / Solution Architect (bare, no vendor-product or consulting/SI qualifier)
known_ambiguous_titles.reason: Both archetypes use the identical base title. This rubric only auto-classifies when a vendor-product token (AWS/Azure/Salesforce/etc.) or a consulting/SI qualifier is present in the title; bare 'Solutions Architect' should be flagged for manual review rather than guessed, especially since company name (the strongest real signal) usually isn't in the title string.

known_ambiguous_titles.pattern: TPM (bare acronym, or 'Technical Product Manager' vs 'Technical Program Manager' unclear from truncated/abbreviated titles)
known_ambiguous_titles.reason: The same 3-letter acronym is used for two genuinely different roles. Do not treat bare 'TPM' as a positive signal for technical-product-manager; require the full spelled-out phrase.

Cloudflare-originated title (and now used more broadly) for a role that is nominally in a support org but staffed/leveled like SRE with proactive reliability engineering as a core function, per customer-support-engineer.md's own boundary-case note.

Meta/Facebook popularized 'Production Engineer' as an SRE-equivalent title, but the identical title is extremely common in manufacturing/industrial/food/beverage contexts for a non-software role. Volume-check this pattern's hit rate against SIC/industry data if available before trusting the auto-classification at scale.

Extremely common non-software title in manufacturing, oil & gas, utilities, and plant-maintenance contexts (reliability-centered maintenance, RCM). Only 'Site Reliability Engineer' is treated as an unambiguous SRE signal in this rubric.

collision: (out-of-taxonomy: IT/network systems engineer, defense/aerospace systems engineering)
known_ambiguous_titles.reason: One of the most overloaded titles in any general job-title corpus -- spans DevOps/platform work, embedded/hardware systems engineering, general IT/network administration, and formal defense-industry 'systems engineering' (requirements/V&V across a whole product). No positive pattern in this rubric matches bare 'Systems Engineer' for exactly this reason.

known_ambiguous_titles.pattern: Technical Project Manager / Engineering Project Manager
collision: (out-of-taxonomy: Technical Program Manager / general project management)
known_ambiguous_titles.reason: Deliberately left unclassified by this rubric (not matched by technical-product-manager's positive_patterns, and carved out of the project-manager always_exclude_pattern via negative lookbehind rather than blanket-excluded). Real usage splits between roadmap/delivery-coordination work (out of scope, PM-not-PdM) and, less commonly, a de facto TPM-equivalent at companies that don't use 'Product Manager' internally. Low enough volume and confidence to warrant manual review rather than a guess in either direction.

Used for both hands-on implementation-delivery roles and more senior design-led consulting-SA roles depending on the firm; consulting-engineer-professional-services.md and solutions-architect-consulting.md both cite overlapping real postings using this exact title.

developer-relations-advocacy.md notes DX-titled roles sit 'under the broader DevRel umbrella,' but in practice this title is also widely used for internal-developer-platform/tooling engineers whose 'customers' are other engineers, not external developers -- a platform-infrastructure-engineer scope. Title text alone doesn't disambiguate internal vs. external audience.

known_ambiguous_titles.pattern: Tech Lead / Team Lead (bare, no 'Engineering Manager' qualifier)
known_ambiguous_titles.reason: Genuinely split in real usage between a senior IC with informal technical leadership duties (still an implementer) and a first-line people manager who has stopped being the primary implementer. The engineering-management.md brief's own defining test (evaluator vs. implementer) isn't reliably signaled by this title alone.

Ranges from a lightly-technical backlog-management role (not a good fit for technical-product-manager's architecture-tradeoff scope) to a role functionally identical to a TPM at some companies. Not confidently mapped to any of the 18 archetypes by title alone.

Onboarding-flavored title that could describe either SOW-scoped technical implementation work (consulting-engineer-professional-services) or account-embedded customer onboarding with lighter technical depth (customer-support-solutions-engineer), depending on the employer.

Extremely overloaded across industries -- telecom/hardware field service (non-software), on-site customer support, and forward-deployed-style embedded delivery all use this exact title. No positive pattern in this rubric matches it.

Cross-company sample showed this means different things per employer: OpenAI/Writer use it as their FDE-equivalent (AI embedded at customer sites); Supabase/Crusoe/Anduril use it for infrastructure/network deployment tooling; robotics companies (Figure AI, Skydio, Path Robotics, Locus Robotics, Applied Intuition) use it for physical robot/hardware site installation. No single archetype fits all cases -- resolved via a company-context override for OpenAI specifically (publicly documented as FDE-modeled), left ambiguous/unclassified elsewhere rather than guessed.

always_exclude_patterns.reason: Non-software engineering disciplines. Adjacency-based (e.g. 'Mechanical Engineer') so does not false-positive on titles like 'Mechanical Design Software Engineer' where 'engineer' isn't immediately adjacent to the discipline word in the exact discipline+engineer bigram.

Bare discipline+engineer titles (non-software). Does not match 'Aerospace Software Engineer' or 'Automotive Embedded Engineer' since those have an intervening software/embedded/firmware token, which correctly routes them to embedded-iot-engineer or product-full-stack-software-engineer instead.
Non-software discipline; does not match 'Electrical/Firmware Engineer' hybrid titles due to adjacency requirement.

No dedicated QA/Test archetype exists among the 18 SWE Genie archetypes. Excluded from all archetypes including the generalist product-full-stack-software-engineer fallback, per the task's own worked example.
Same rationale as QA/Test Engineer -- SEIT/SDET-family titles have no home in this 18-archetype taxonomy.
Software Development Engineer in Test acronym -- same QA/test exclusion rationale.
Physical security, not software security engineering.
Physical security, not software security engineering.

Offensive-security work; security-engineer.md's own scope note explicitly frames this archetype as defensive/proactive-posture, 'not primarily offensive/hacking work.' No dedicated offensive-security archetype exists in v1.
Offensive-security work, same rationale as Penetration Tester above.

Explicitly decided OUT of v1 scope in roles-summary.md's Phase 2 resolution ('Data Scientist / Data Analyst -- in or out of v1... Decision: OUT of v1, added to the v2 backlog'), on the grounds that this population is frequently entered from non-SWE backgrounds, breaking the product's core 'lateral move from a software engineering base' framing.

Explicitly and deliberately excluded from ml-engineer.md as a PhD-gated, publication-oriented role out of scope for this software-engineer-facing taxonomy. Does not match if 'Engineer' is the terminal word (e.g. a hybrid 'Research Engineer' title with clear production-shipping scope would need separate human judgment; this pattern targets the bare Scientist-suffixed title).

Generic, non-technical project management. 'Technical Project Manager' / 'Engineering Project Manager' are excluded from this always-exclude pattern via negative lookbehind since those are closer to the TPM/program-manager boundary and arguably more software-adjacent -- treat as known_ambiguous rather than confidently mapping them into technical-product-manager, but do not blanket-exclude them either.
Agile process-facilitation role, not a software engineering archetype.
Agile process-facilitation role, not a software engineering archetype.
Pure HR/recruiting function, including technical recruiters -- explicitly out of scope for an engineering-career-archetype taxonomy.
Pure HR function.
Finance function, unrelated to any of the 18 engineering archetypes.
Marketing function, unrelated to any of the 18 engineering archetypes.
Retail/hourly-labor role.
Hourly-labor / non-engineering role that occasionally appears in mixed multi-vertical ATS harvests (e.g. a retailer's public ATS board mixed with its engineering postings).

Pure (non-technical) sales roles without 'engineer' in the title -- these should never fall through to any engineering archetype, including sales-engineer-pre-sales, which requires the word 'engineer'.
